items.prompt: During stable in-place compaction, what does the write index represent after the first read positions have been processed?

options.text: The number of retained values already written into the valid output prefix.
The position of the next unread input value.
Only the number of removed values.
The physical length of the array.

feedback.reason: The write index is the boundary between the valid compacted prefix and unused positions.
feedback.details: After processing a prefix of the input, positions [0, write) contain exactly the retained values from that processed prefix in their original relative order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.next_unread_position: The read index, not the write index, tracks the next input position to inspect.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.number_removed_only: Removed count can be derived from read and write, but it is not the direct meaning of write.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.physical_array_length: Compaction usually changes the logical result length without changing physical storage length.

items.prompt: A filter keeps values satisfying a predicate. When should the write index advance?

options.text: Only after a retained value has been written to array[write].
On every read iteration, whether the value is retained or removed.
Only when the current value is removed.
Only once after the scan finishes.

feedback.reason: Write counts retained output positions, so it advances exactly when one output value is produced.
feedback.details: If a value is removed, no new valid output position is created. Incrementing write anyway would leave gaps or include stale values in the logical prefix.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.every_read_iteration: This makes write track processed input rather than retained output.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_when_value_removed: Removed values should not consume positions in the compacted prefix.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.once_after_loop: The algorithm needs the current output boundary during the scan.

items.prompt: Which invariant prevents stable compaction from overwriting unread input?

options.text: At every step, write <= read.
At every step, read <= write.
At every step, write equals n.
At every step, read equals write + 1.

feedback.reason: The output boundary can lag behind the read position but must never move ahead of it.
feedback.details: Writing to an index at or before the current read position cannot destroy future unread values. If write moved beyond read, the algorithm could overwrite input that has not yet been examined.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.read_not_ahead_of_write: This permits write to move into unread territory.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.write_equals_length: Write should reflect retained count, which can be much smaller than n.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.read_equals_write_plus_one: Read and write may be equal for long stretches when all values are retained.

items.prompt: A filter removes every input value. What should the final write index be?

options.text: n

feedback.reason: No retained values means the valid compacted prefix has length zero.
feedback.details: Read still scans the entire input, but write never advances because no output value is produced.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one: The algorithm does not reserve a placeholder output position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_minus_one: This confuses an index with the number of retained values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n: Processed input count and retained output count are different.

items.prompt: Why does stable compaction preserve the relative order of retained values?

options.text: Read moves left to right, and each retained value is written to the next output position.
The retained prefix is sorted after the scan.
Each removed value is swapped with the final element.
Removing duplicates automatically preserves order.

feedback.reason: Values are emitted in the same order in which they are encountered.
feedback.details: The write index increases monotonically, so later retained values cannot be placed before earlier retained values.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_after_scan: Sorting would impose a new order rather than preserve the original one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.swap_with_end: Swapping from the end can change the relative order of retained values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_removed: Duplicate behavior and stability are separate contract dimensions.

items.prompt: Arrange the operations for one iteration of stable compaction when the current value should be retained.

elements.text: Read the current value at the read index.
Evaluate whether the value should be retained.
Write the retained value at the write index.
Advance the write index.

feedback.reason: The decision must be based on the source value before the output boundary advances.
feedback.details: Writing occurs only after the predicate accepts the value, and write advances only after a valid output position has been filled.

items.prompt: Which statements are valid invariants during stable in-place compaction? Select all that apply.

options.text: Positions [0, write) contain exactly the retained values from the processed input prefix.
write never exceeds the number of input positions already processed.
The retained values in [0, write) remain in original relative order.
Every position at or after write already contains its final required value.

feedback.reason: The invariant describes the completed prefix and the safe relation between read and write.
feedback.details: Only [0, write) is guaranteed to be valid output during the scan. The suffix may still contain unread input or stale values.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.suffix_already_final: The suffix is not part of the established output invariant and may change later.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.valid_prefix: This is the semantic meaning of the write boundary.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.write_le_read: It prevents writes from moving into unread input.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.order_preserved: Stable left-to-right emission preserves relative order.

items.prompt: A stable in-place filter scans n elements, uses read and write indices, and returns the logical length k. Select its time, auxiliary-space, and additional output-space complexity.
constraints: Every input element is inspected once.
The result is stored in the existing array prefix.

feedback.reason: The scan is linear and reuses the input array as the logical output buffer.
feedback.details: Read visits all n positions. A constant number of indices gives O(1) auxiliary space, and no growing result container is allocated outside the input.

items.prompt: When read equals write and the current value is retained, is assigning array[write] = array[read] correct?

options.text: Yes. It is a harmless self-assignment and preserves the invariant.
No. Equal read and write indices are always out of bounds.
No. write must not advance when read equals write.
No. The value must be swapped with the final element.

feedback.reason: Writing a retained value to its existing position is semantically correct.
feedback.details: An implementation may optionally avoid the assignment as a micro-optimization, but it must still advance write because one output value was retained.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_out_of_bounds: Equal indices can be any valid processed position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_skip_write_and_increment_read_only: Skipping the write is harmless, but failing to advance write breaks the retained-count invariant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_swap_with_end: End swaps are unnecessary and can violate stability.

items.prompt: A buggy filter increments write on every iteration but writes only retained values. What failure can occur?

options.text: The logical prefix can contain gaps or stale values where removed elements consumed write positions.
The read index automatically stops before the end.
The array is accidentally sorted.
Every duplicate is automatically removed.

feedback.reason: Write positions must correspond one-to-one with retained outputs.
feedback.details: Advancing write for removed values falsely expands the valid prefix without placing a valid retained value at that position.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.read_stops_early: The write bug does not change the read loop boundary by itself.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_always_removed: The bug concerns output placement, not duplicate detection.

items.prompt: Why is it safe to overwrite array[write] while scanning left to right?

options.text: write is never ahead of read, so the destination lies in the processed region.
Array values cannot actually be overwritten.
All unread values have already been copied to another collection.
Sorting protects unread values from modification.

feedback.reason: The read/write invariant keeps writes behind the frontier of unread input.
feedback.details: A destination index less than read has already been consumed. A destination equal to read is a self-write of the current value.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_values_immutable: In-place compaction explicitly mutates the array.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unread_values_copied_elsewhere: The method is designed to avoid a growing backup collection.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_protects_values: Sorting is unrelated and may violate the required order.

items.prompt: Arrange the checks for debugging an incorrect final logical length in a read/write compaction algorithm.

elements.text: State that write equals the number of retained values.
Trace every location where write is incremented.
Test inputs where none and all values are retained.
Verify that [0, write) contains exactly write valid outputs.

feedback.reason: Debugging should start from the semantic meaning of the boundary and then verify every state transition.
feedback.details: The all-retained and none-retained cases expose unconditional or missing increments. The final prefix check confirms that the numeric boundary matches actual valid content.

items.prompt: Which implementation choices preserve stable compaction semantics? Select all that apply.

options.text: Scan source positions from left to right.
Write only retained values to consecutive output positions.
Return write as the logical result length.
Swap each removed value with the last unprocessed value.

feedback.reason: Stability requires left-to-right emission into consecutive output positions.
feedback.details: The final write index defines the valid prefix. Swapping from the end can reorder retained values and therefore violates stable semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.swap_removed_with_end: This may be useful for unstable removal but can change relative order.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.left_to_right_read: Encounter order is the source order that stability must preserve.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.write_only_retained: Consecutive retained writes create a gap-free logical prefix.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.return_write: The caller needs the exclusive boundary of the valid compacted result.

items.prompt: A sorted array is compacted in place to keep one copy of each distinct value. Select the time, auxiliary-space, and additional output-space complexity.
constraints: Only read and write indices are used.
The result is the existing array prefix.

feedback.reason: Each input position is scanned once, and the compacted result reuses existing storage.
feedback.details: The write boundary advances only for a new retained value. No collection grows with n outside the input array.

items.prompt: A removal algorithm swaps every unwanted value with the last active element and shrinks the active range. Why is this not a stable compaction algorithm?

options.text: A value from the end can move before retained values that originally preceded it.
Any algorithm with two indices is unstable.
The method works only when duplicates exist.
It necessarily allocates O(n) auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: Stable compaction must preserve the original relative order of all retained values.
feedback.details: Swapping from the end can be correct when order is irrelevant, but it changes the positional sequence and therefore violates a stable output contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.uses_two_indices: Index count does not determine stability; update rules do.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.requires_duplicates: The issue is removal and order, not duplicate presence.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_linear_space: The method can be in place and still be unstable.

items.prompt: A compaction function returns write - 1 instead of write. What is the likely defect?

options.text: It returns the last valid index rather than the logical output length.
It always reports one extra retained value.
It forces the compacted prefix to become sorted.
It changes auxiliary space from O(1) to O(n).

feedback.reason: Write is the exclusive end of the valid prefix and therefore already equals its length.
feedback.details: Subtracting one converts the boundary into the final index. It also produces -1 for an empty result, which is not the logical length.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.returns_too_many: Subtracting one underreports rather than overreports the retained count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.forces_sorting: The return expression does not reorder values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.changes_aux_space: A boundary arithmetic error does not allocate additional memory.

items.prompt: A character array should be normalized in place by removing punctuation while preserving all letters, digits, duplicates, and relative order. Which approach matches the contract?

options.text: Scan left to right and write each retained character at the next write position.
Sort all characters, then remove punctuation.
Insert retained characters into a set and write the set back.
Swap punctuation with characters from the end until punctuation is outside the active range.

feedback.reason: A stable read/write filter removes only ignored characters while preserving every retained occurrence in order.
feedback.details: Sorting, set conversion, and end swaps each destroy at least one required property: order or multiplicity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_then_remove: Sorting changes the relative order of retained characters.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.set_of_characters: A set removes duplicate retained characters and does not preserve sequence order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.swap_with_end: End swaps can reorder retained characters.

items.prompt: Arrange the state transitions for one iteration when the current value is rejected by a stable compaction predicate.

elements.text: Read the current source value.
Determine that the predicate rejects it.
Leave the write boundary unchanged.
Advance to the next source position.

feedback.reason: A rejected value consumes input but produces no output position.
feedback.details: Read advances because the source occurrence has been processed. Write remains unchanged because the valid prefix gains no retained value.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning that proves the read/write invariant is preserved when a value is retained.

elements.text: Assume [0, write) already contains the retained values from the processed prefix.
Write the accepted current value at array[write].
Increment write so the valid prefix includes the new value.
Confirm that relative order is preserved and write does not move ahead of the next read boundary.

feedback.reason: The proof follows the invariant before the iteration, the state update, and the invariant after the update.
feedback.details: Appending the current retained value to the established prefix preserves both content and order, while the boundary relation keeps unread input safe.

items.prompt: Arrange the finalization steps after the read index has processed the entire input.

elements.text: Stop when read reaches n.
Treat [0, write) as the complete compacted result.
Leave positions at or after write outside the logical output contract.
Return write as the logical result length.

feedback.reason: Completion freezes the established output prefix and reports its exclusive boundary.
feedback.details: The suffix need not be cleared unless explicitly required. Returning write exposes exactly the number of retained values.
